data.title: Dino Polska: Real Problem Or Opportunity?
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Dino Polska (DNOPY)(DNOPF) was in June, to analyze its Q1 results. Since then, the stock has not performed as I expected, clearly underperforming the index, even though the results were not bad and it was executing well on its long-term plan.

My goal with today's post is to analyze what has happened during this time and try to explain the more than 30% drop since the historical highs set, precisely, shortly after the publication of my article.

Dino is a curious company. It only reports quarterly and does not do analyst conferences. This can be attractive for those who do not have much time to follow their portfolio companies, especially for the peace of mind that comes from having the founding CEO at the head of the company (51% of outstanding shares), without any media appearances and only focused on executing the expansion and business plan. This little communication can only happen in companies with such a CEO.

Thesis Review

This is a fragment from my previous article that I believe summarizes the company very well.

Let's briefly recall the thesis for Dino Polska. Dino is a Polish supermarket chain with a strong presence in the western part of the country. It's characterized by having a founder-led management, high vertical integration, ownership of its store locations and the land it operates on, as well as its own meat processing plants and distribution centers.

The company's main competitive advantage lies in its "local monopolies" in rural areas of the country. By focusing mainly on towns with no more than 5,000 inhabitants and maintaining a high level of agility in building stores (the company owns the firm that constructs its supermarkets), Dino makes it unprofitable for other competitors to enter these market niches.

If we recall, Dino's long-term bullish thesis is based on its ability to continue expanding the number of stores in Poland (currently 2,688) while maintaining organic growth above inflation. We will see how well this combination holds up, but it should drive sales growth in the mid-single-digit range. With good capital allocation, bottom-line growth should be in the high-double-digit range. Let's review its figures.

Earnings Review

If I say hardly anything about Q2, it is because it is like reading a carbon copy of Q3, in fact, the documents presented by the company are practically identical.

What I would highlight most from Q2, is the explanation for the increase in personnel costs (+22.7% YoY) that appeared in the earnings report:

This growth was a consequence of the higher number of Dino Group employees, from 45,274 as of June 30, 2024 to 53,523 as of June 30, 2025, in connection with the expansion of the size of the Dino Group business, the expansion of the Dino store network, the increase in LFL sales in existing stores and the higher average salary within the Dino Group.

Going back to more recent times, on November 6, Dino presented Q3 earnings.

The first thing I would highlight, one of the pillars of the investment thesis, is that the pace of new store openings has accelerated again (98 vs. 68 in Q3 2024). It already has 2,933 stores in the country (2,774 have their own solar panels, increasing their energy self-sufficiency).

This is good news, especially considering the potential TAM that Dino has, which is estimated to be at least 7,000 stores within Poland. This is undoubtedly one of the company's main growth drivers and the main reason behind its capital reinvestment (more on this later).

It is important to consider that the maximum levels of store maturity and profitability are reached after 4-5 years. So we are probably still a long way from seeing Dino's true returns.

The other driver of sales is organic store growth, the so-called LFL. This growth has to be compared to inflation in order to arrive at the real growth of Dino stores.

Gone are the days of stellar store growth of almost 30%. It is normal; when inflation falls, organic growth decreases, and even more so when it is a low-cost operator.

If inflation is higher than store LFLs, real growth ends up being negative. Unfortunately, this is starting to become more common than in the past. The erratic behaviors of the last two quarters are due to the Easter vacations falling in different quarters in 2023 vs. 2024.

And, frankly, I think this is the metric that is currently moving Dino's share price.

LFLs this year are being chaotic. Two quarters of growth below inflation and one way above due to a change at Easter week from last year. The board doesn't have much communication with the investor. I think it is unclear what a normalized year would have looked like and if all quarters would have been below inflation in the absence of the calendar change.

The fusion of poor communication, with less liquidity than in other countries, is what leads this stock to suffer such large (30%) declines on an ongoing basis. Or at least this is my theory.

What should reassure shareholders is knowing that the founding CEO is behind it and that they are still executing in an accelerated manner this year, so perhaps this will turn out to be a one-off this year and really have little impact on LP's thesis. In the valuation part we'll see....

The combination of these two metrics gives us the company's sales growth, a good 15.17%, although we have already seen that it has been completely driven by the increase in the number of stores.

What has surprised me for the better is the remarkable increase in the EBITDA margin to 8.6%. Anyway, the same quarter last year was 8.9%. It is curious how there is a clear seasonality in this figure, without any really clear explanation. In 2023 history repeats itself.

The reasons for this quarter fall within the normal and expected range:

  • Better cost control (lower growth than sales).
  • Fresh products (such as meat and sausages) accounted for 40.2% of sales in Q3 2025, compared to 38.6% in Q3 2024. These products are more profitable, especially since Dino produces part of them internally (Agro-Rydzyna plant).
  • The new logistics center in Brzeg completed construction in Q3, improving distribution efficiency (although it opens in Q4).

Although at the annual level there are more compelling reasons. The main factor behind this margin compression has been lower product prices and, above all, higher labor costs. This category of spending has experienced a lagged effect, as final price inflation hit the country in 2022 and 2023, but wages did not increase significantly until 2024.

On the other hand, the company's financial position is already almost net cash. We should see savings in Interest Expenses.

Capital Allocation

Here lies one of the most important points of Dino's thesis.

Like any good supermarket, Dino Polska has a Negative Working Capital. This generally makes the OCF higher than the NOPAT.

Historically, Dino has not generated positive FCF, because it reinvested all cash in CAPEX to open stores.

The previous two years have been quite exceptional, as there was positive FCF in both years because, for the first time, they did not invest all the cash in CAPEX. This made me think that maybe Dino could start implementing a policy of returning capital to the shareholder, but we can see that, for the moment, it will have to wait.

It is fine for a company to reinvest all its cash, as long as it has lines of business with interesting ROICs (higher than WACC).

Dino's historical returns (CROICs), as shown in the picture, have been 30% and ROCEs of 15%. If it reinvests 100% of the cash at these rates of return, the future bottom-line growth of the business should not stray far from these growth rates (25.9% CAGR in the last 6 years).

This year they have reaccelerated store openings, reinvestment opportunities have returned, and FCF has fizzled again, but I wouldn't worry; we'll see what metrics are used to value this company later. Dino Polska is expected to close 2025 with between 320 and 330 new openings, rates not seen since 2021-2022.

Anyway, this has an impact on working capital... Increase in inventories: Dino has expanded its store network (+245 new stores in 2025), which implies more initial stock for openings. If last year it had a cash inflow of 345M ZLTs, this year, so far, it has an outflow of 50M ZLTs.

And this is how Dino's capital reinvestment and value creation process works, which is a textbook example of a compounder company.

Valuation

Dino does not buy back shares or distribute dividends, so our yield as shareholders, at least on that side, is 0%.

Moreover, as FCF is negative, we will have to focus on the growth of a metric that replaces FCF, EBITDA. In Dino's case, this is the metric that the market looks at the most.

Estimating Dino's growth is actually quite simple. We have to play with 3 things:

  • Rate of openings
  • LFL growth
  • EBITDA margin

I originally thought the way to do this was to project a percentage on the growth rate of these stores, but if you do that, you end up with a larger number opening each year, which is unlikely in reality.

So I think the best thing to do is to play around with the number of stores we think you can open per year. I think this number will be between 300 and 350. This leaves us with growth rates of between 9.4% and 10.4% on this metric.

Then we have to see what LFL growth rate Dino is capable of. I think it will be closer to 4% than anything else. We have seen before how, as inflation falls in Poland, this number falls as well.

In the lowest scenario, which I will use, it gives us sales growth of 13.9% until 2029.

Now we have to see what EBITDA margin the company may be able to achieve in 2029. If we have lower sales growth, there may be less operating leverage, so I have decided to increase the margin to only 8.5% in 2029. Maybe it will end up being 9%, who knows, but I say I want to make a conservative case.

With this, we get 15.5% growth in EBITDA.

The exit multiple chosen is 15X EBITDA, which, from the 38 ZLTs that I find interesting, gives us an IRR of 17.6% in the most base case of all to 2029.

Moreover, this average double-digit return would be achieved in the most conservative case of all. We could put the opening rate at 350, LFL at 5%, and EBITDA margin at 9%, for a return of 22%. In addition, this could lead to a higher multiple, increasing our shareholder return.

For all these reasons, I rate the stock as a buy.

Conclusion

Dino may be an interesting investment idea away from the AI boom. A company that is easy to understand, with a clear execution plan, and does not require a lot of follow-up work. The thesis is clear: just let the CEO execute.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: STEW: Discounted Valuation Justifies A Buy
data.description:
Overview

While ETFs are usually the most popular approach for investors to get diversity across equities, this may not be the best approach for some investors. An index like the S&P 500 has a lot of companies that investors may not be excited to hold. This is where a more concentrated approach like the SRH Total Return Fund (STEW) comes into play. STEW takes a more focused approach to its portfolio and management selects what it believes to be the highest quality assets based on its bottom-up value driven selection process. When I previously covered STEW, I issued a buy rating due to the tax-efficient nature of the distributions. However, I wanted to revisit the fund's performance and value proposition as we approach the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that STEW's share price has increased by about 7.9%. This demonstrates the fund's ability to participate in the positive momentum of the market rally over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, STEW's total return jumps up to 11.4% over the same time frame. STEW now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 4.5% while offering attractive dividend growth over a longer holding period. This also makes the fund an attractive choice for investors seeking a source of traditional dividend growth.

Additionally, the fund continues to trade at a deeply discounted price to NAV valuation. This can be an indication that it's an attractive time to accumulate shares on its current weakness. While there are some risks to consider, such as underperformance against peers, the fund is still a great long-term position for investors looking to get the best of both worlds: price growth and a growing stream of dividend income. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that STEW implements.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, STEW has total managed assets of $2.44B that are allocated across 23 different equity positions. Management's strategy is to identify investment opportunities that will provide attractive returns over a long period of time. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 1.14%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes other fees related to operating expenses. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits closer to 0.99%.

Despite its focused approach to holdings, the fund has an efficient level of diversity across different industries. As we can see below, STEW is most heavily allocated to Diversified investments at 36.87%, which represents businesses that have exposure to a diverse range of sectors. This is followed by an allocation to Diversified Financial Services and Consumer Services, which accounts for 22.85% and 5.13% of assets respectively. However, the focus on the top two industries presents a degree of concentration risk if the Financial industry experiences headwinds.

When looking closer at the holdings, it makes sense why the fund is so focused on a small industry concentration. Since the top holding is Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), it's clear why STEW's breakdown by industry is most heavily allocated to Diversified investments. BRK.B essentially provides indirect exposure to some of the greatest companies in the world.

Fellow analyst, James Foord, published an article highlighting BRK.B's latest 13F. However, we can see that STEW also provides direct exposure to high quality businesses including JPMorgan (JPM), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Microsoft (MSFT), and Evercore (EVR), just to name a few. Therefore, STEW is an easy way to instantly gain exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world, while trimming the fat of indexes like the S&P 500. However, the top ten holdings make up over 88% of total assets, which does present concentration risk.

Performance Insights & Comparison

However, investors need to understand that STEW's higher dividend yield means that its performance may have some limitations. For instance, we can refer to the long-term performance of the fund below. As we can see, STEW's total return sits at 243% over the last ten years. Despite the fund's higher starting dividend yield, the fund still underperforms a more traditional index-tracking ETF like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) over the same time frame. Since STEW prioritizes income generation, the fund has actively sold off portions of the upside price movement of its holdings. Therefore, the price growth of STEW is ultimately limited compared to traditional growth focused ETFs.

Additionally, there are several closed-end funds that aim to provide attractive total returns through a portfolio of equities. While their underlying selection process for its holdings and breakdown by sector may be different, the underlying objective to provide attractive total returns while providing a high dividend yield remains similar. I believe that the following two funds are the most compelling peers out there:

  • Virtus Total Return Fund (ZTR): 9.5% dividend yield.
  • Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ): 6.3% dividend yield.

As we can see below, CSQ is the largest in terms of assets under management at $3.3B. CSQ's standout feature is that it utilizes a blend of both equities and debt securities. However, STEW has the longest operating history that dates back to 1972, which indicates a long track record of success and management's ability to adapt to different market cycles. Conversely, ZTR is the smallest fund in terms of assets under management and also has the shortest operating history with an inception dating back to 2005. ZTR's standout feature is that it takes a global approach to its assets, with exposure to equities that have primarily operations outside of the US.

When running a performance comparison with this peer group, we can see that CSQ is the winner here. Over the last ten years, CSQ has the highest total return of 345.8% when including distributions paid. This means that CSQ significantly outperforms STEW's total return of 243% over the same time frame. However, STEW's underlying strategy demonstrates the largest level of price growth potential of the peer group. STEW's higher price growth can likely be attributed to the fact that the fund simply has the lowest dividend yield of the peer group.

Despite the fund's underperformance against CSQ, a case can still be made for the value proposition of STEW at this time. Since STEW is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, STEW now trades at a large discount to NAV of 21.1%. For reference, STEW has traded at an average discount to NAV of 18.81% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the chart below, we can see that STEW's current discount to NAV sits at the larger end of its ten-year range. This can be an indication that it's a good time to accumulate shares on the weakness.

However, the recent price to NAV discount may be attributed to the fact that Berkshire Hathaway's performance has remained lackluster compared to the indexes. Just as a reference point, we can see how BRK.B has underperformed SPY and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) over the last three-year period.

Dividend Strength

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.20 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 4.5%. This latest payout represents a sizeable 21.2% raise from the prior quarter's distribution of $0.165 per share. When it comes to a reliable source of income generation, STEW is a great pick for investors seeking stability. The fund is an outlier amongst peer closed-end funds because STEW has provided a sense of traditional dividend growth in addition to its high starting dividend yield. For instance, STEW has raised its dividend at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 10.10% over the last five years, which is extremely impressive.

Since the fund has historically demonstrated no issues generating earnings that exceed the distributions, the fund is likely to continue raising dividends over time. This makes STEW a great choice for investors that want to see their annual passive income grow, without the need to consistently add new capital. Additionally, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the payouts are issued in a tax-efficient manner, which limits the overall tax burden for investors. This is achieved through the usage of net realized long-term gains and return of capital, which have favorable tax treatment for investors.

Just to demonstrate the strength of the fund's dividend raises, I ran a back test of an original $10,000 investment at the start of 2016. This back test assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $10K. It also assumes that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. As we can see below, investors would have been able to increase their annual dividend income by simply remaining invested:

2016 Dividend Income: $527

2025 Dividend Income: $1,221

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on STEW at this time due to the attractive price to NAV valuation. The fund continues to trade at a compelling price to NAV discount, which may be attributed to Berkshire Hathaway's recent weakness. Despite this, I believe that this price disconnect presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors. The fund still has an excellent history of dividend raises over time, while also prioritizing tax-efficient distributions for shareholders. The fund takes a more focused approach than traditional index-tracking ETFs, which may be an efficient way to trim the fluff of the index.

data.title: PAR Technology: The Reset Is Done, Now Execution Decides The Stock
data.description:
PAR Technology Corporation's (PAR) valuation reset earlier this year is what pulled me back in. The stock now trades near 4x revenue, and Q3 showed enough progress for me to stay constructive. ARR reached about $298 million, up 22% YoY, and subscription revenue grew 25 percent to roughly $74.8 million, so we can see a steadier run rate for the business.

Moving forward, my focus is on how the stock responds if PAR keeps rolling out new sites faster and manages to maintain mid-teens organic ARR (annual recurring revenue) growth. I believe the stock price has dropped steeply over the last few months because the rate of deployment with big customers like Burger King and Popeyes hasn’t been on schedule. That should change over the next quarter or so, and the company is also doing a good job with its attach rates.

I’m still bullish on the company’s fundamentals driving the upside in the near term, even with all the noise.

The Two Things PAR Must Get Right From Here

The real question for me now is how quickly PAR can turn the contracted demand into live sites. ARR looks good on paper, but the stock will only work if implementations move at a steady pace. I kept thinking about the Burger King pause earlier in the year because it showed the entire tension in one moment. The company already had the contract, but it was rather difficult to roll out POS, loyalty, and data together. However, we’ve had constant messaging from management that it has been working through those issues all year, and the tone across the last two earnings calls suggests onboarding is in a better place heading into 2026.

Following on from that, the speed at which PAR can implement at its sites is crucial because the economics per site are now stronger than before. As I mentioned in the introduction, Q3 ARR came in at about $298 million, up 22%, and the quality in the wins is evident with the attach rates. Every new POS customer in the quarter took at least one other product, usually loyalty or data, and payments attach continued to improve. That tells me customers are leaning into the platform, not treating Brink as a stand-alone system.

We must also consider how durable its ARR is. You cannot reach mid-teens organic growth without stable renewals and deeper relationships with your existing customers, and PAR is executing well on that front. Its subscription revenue reached roughly $74.8 million, and total revenue grew 23% YoY to $119.2 million.

To be clear, the company still needs to handle concentration risk. RBI is its biggest customer, so anything that happens with that relationship affects the narrative far more than any other group. I like that the partnership is expanding, but the downside is partly what we’ve seen this year with the swings in rollouts.

Finally, it has improved its general financial and operational metrics. EBITDA and cash flow are up, so the inference is that we’re seeing the heavy investment cycle slowly ending. All things considered, Q3 has reinforced my confidence in PAR’s trajectory.

Why the Price Gives Me Room, and What Could Still Test My Patience

The reset is the major reason I am even more comfortable staying constructive here. PAR now trades around 4x revenue, which is a very different setup from the 7.5x range that made me call a Hold back in June. It is still fairly close to the multiples we saw in September, when I upgraded my rating to a buy. At this level, the stock still carries all the baggage from the operational noise that has been present all year, without accounting for the company’s medium- and long-term prospects.

In my opinion, PAR deserves to sit above NCR on both multiples above because its recurring mix and the unit economics are fundamentally different. Sure, they serve similar markets but in different areas, and NCR is a much bigger company. With Toast, it’s a bit harder to justify a premium based on their forward growth rates, but PAR has better gross margins. And if it can keep its deployments on schedule and maintain consistent ARR growth, then we should see a rerating over the near term.

Having said that, we still need to keep an eye on a few things. I already touched on the customer concentration risk and rollout execution, and they affect PAR’s margin development. Yes, its subscription service margin is great (25% higher YoY), but hardware is still weighing on the consolidated margins. The company needs to keep improving its operating leverage via software and payments so that the overall figure goes up.

Besides that, the company trades in a segment where sentiment can shift pretty quickly. We might see the stock pull back again if SaaS multiples across the board start to compress, so it’s not a company-specific flaw, but I thought to point it out.

Then, TASK. Management is deferring TASK rollout and revenue to 2026 so it can chase the bigger international chains. I think the tradeoff makes sense strategically, but it also comes with timing risks. As investors, it’s normal to want to see the first meaningful Tier 1 deployment before we assign a value to that pipeline. So, we’re left waiting on TASK as a 2026 story, not a near-term growth story.

If PAR Executes, The Stock Will Rerate

I see PAR in a far healthier position now than it was at the beginning of this year, even though the valuation is more reflective of the noise around the company at several points over the last few quarters. Generally speaking, the company is doing better in terms of its operations, and I don’t think profitability is too far off. Given all these factors, I’m confident that the stock will rise again once we see better execution heading into 2026.

data.title: IIF: Historical Outperformance And Superior Industry Approach
data.description:
MS India Investment Fund Outlook

Indian equities have been on a tear in the past decade, and while the economy and stock market still have a favorable outlook ahead of them, many investors are concerned about valuations and economic risks this year.

As a result, Indian equities have lost steam this year and have had flat performance, while other Asian stock markets like South Korea have been some of the main drivers of the favorable performance of emerging markets.

India still has a lot of appeal going for it. Economic growth will outpace that of many emerging market peers, and the Indian consumer will be a strong driver of the country's growth, while export performance may continue to gyrate.

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund (IIF) looks like a good vehicle to accumulate on any potential dips in the coming months, as this would result in an even deeper discount to NAV. This fund invests in some of the leading companies in India, including favorable-performing industries like banking and consumer-themed industries.

MS India Investment Fund Overview

The MS India Investment Fund is a closed-end fund that utilizes a top-down and bottom-up approach to invest in equities in India. It invests in many of the dominant large-cap companies in indices such as the MSCI India Index.

IIF is a relatively smaller fund with around $280 million in assets, which has a 1.31% net expense ratio.

One appeal of this closed-end fund is that it trades at a 10% discount to NAV, which is slightly more than its historical average. IIF is a great fund to take advantage of during pullbacks in the markets, as this discount can often widen.

The MS India Investment Fund has been a clear victor in terms of relative performance, trouncing the MSCI India Index since its inception in the 1990s.

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund has also outperformed other India ETFs, including those that focus on small-caps as well as other ETFs that track the MSCI India Index.

Moving forward, IIF looks like one of the most favorable India funds out there, but it may be more vulnerable to sharper drawdowns during any market corrections, as many closed-end funds are.

One of the strong appeals of this fund is its massive exposure to financial and consumer companies, which collectively account for around 70% of its total assets.

The Indian consumer is one of the strongest consumers in Asia, and areas like banking have witnessed substantial growth due to rising penetration rates.

  • Loan growth in India has been over 11% between 2012 and 2025. This high-growth environment will continue to support banks.
  • India's urban population is growing very rapidly and is projected to account for 40% of the population by 2030
  • According to India's Retail Association, retail sales recently grew by 5% and are projected to pick up to 9-10%.

IIF’s top 10 holdings account for around 50% of the fund’s assets. It invests in some of the country's leading banks, as well as other dominant consumer companies.

One appeal of this strategy is that you can invest in some of India’s leading large-cap companies, which major indices track, at a 10+% discount to NAV. The MSCI India Index invests in many of these same companies.

However, this index has a lower weighting in financials and consumer companies, as these industries only account for around 48% of the index.

India's Economic Outlook

India's economy has been experiencing favorable growth despite trade war tensions, which have hit its exports. Consumer growth will continue to support the economy, and bank credit growth remains favorable. With inflation under control, India's central bank is also in a better position to cut rates.

Stronger Recent Growth: India’s economy had one of its strongest quarters recently despite concerns over tariffs. Despite the impact of partial 50% tariffs from the United States, India’s economy still grew by 8.2% last quarter. This is a massive improvement from Q3 2024, when growth was only 5.4%.

India Quarterly GDP Growth Reaches New High

Steady PMI: Manufacturing growth in India has continued to remain very healthy, with PMI readings near 60 in recent months. India’s manufacturing PMI and IIP growth have both remained very favorable. Massive growth in FDI in manufacturing in the past decade has been a massive driver of the industry's growth.

Inflation is Under Control: Inflation has been on a massive downtrend since it peaked in 2021 and 2022. The lower GST for food and other measures have helped ease inflation and improve consumer sentiment. Food inflation, including in rural areas, has also been on a massive downtrend since 2024.

Debt Is Poised to Improve: One major concern rising among emerging markets is the rising sovereign debt levels, which are projected to continue increasing this decade in many emerging markets. Although its debt levels are considerably high, India’s public debt is poised to improve in the coming years, eventually allowing it to fall below the emerging market average.

Consumer Sentiment: The consumer market appears very healthy and will continue to be a major driver of growth in the coming years, helping to offset weaker export growth. This should bode well for ETFs like this that are focused on financial and consumer companies.

Peer ETFs and Final Thoughts

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund has outperformed peer ETFs that track the MSCI India Index and has also outperformed Indian small caps. However, the dominant driver behind this rally in Indian equities, which this fund partially captures, has been the rapid growth of Indian consumer stocks.

The Columbia India Consumer ETF (INCO) outperformed this ETF by over 40 percentage points during this 12+ year period.

The biggest concern with Indian equity markets now is their massive premium to emerging markets. In this environment, it is best to ensure that you are accessing some of the high-growth consumer themes to justify the premium to other emerging Asian markets.

MSCI India

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund looks like a better bet than other funds that track the MSCI India Index, especially since it has a higher weighting in consumer stocks and has a historical benchmark of outperforming. In this environment, it is best to accumulate this fund on any pullbacks as its discount to NAV potentially widens.

India's economic outlook still appears very strong, despite the export shocks of this year, and equities look like a reasonable hold, with the main risk being a mean reversion in valuation.

data.title: Radcom: Reasonably Priced If It Continues To Grow This Way
data.description:
Introduction

Radcom’s (RDCM) shares seem to be returning to life recently, so I wanted to take a look at why that may have been and whether it would be a good time to jump in. The company has been growing steadily in all the most important metrics to me; however, given its massive reliance on AI transformation by telecoms, I am not particularly keen on adding it to my portfolio right now.

The Company

To put it as simply as I can, Radcom builds software that helps mobile operators look into what is happening on their networks at all times, in real-time. With the integration of AI and ML models, the company’s software called RADCOM ACE helps to fix any sort of arising problems automatically. The company’s business model is to sell this software, which connects directly to a telecom network, monitoring the activity of calls, apps, and data.

Revenues are derived through the licensing of the software and subscriptions, and 5G deployments at many different operators.

Recent Performance

Looking at the company’s top-line performance over the last two years, we can see a decent upward trajectory. The growth has been consistent and respectable at around 16% growth per quarter on average recently, which is quite good. It seems that the performance can largely be attributed to strong demand for AI-powered, cloud-native 5G assurance from its Tier-1 customers and other selective wins. The company is riding the AI hype story, and I don’t see a reason not to right now.

Being a software company, its gross margins have been great and have still been improving over the last two years. It’s also interesting to see that its bottom line consistently comes above its EBIT margin, due to the company holding a massive pile of cash and short-term investments on its books, which I will touch on shortly. I was also quite surprised initially that, despite mid-75% gross margins, the company only manages to get around 10% in operating margins. Then, I looked into the company’s financial statements a bit deeper and saw that it has been spending a considerable amount on R&D and SG&A expenses, which are 27% and 36% of revenues, respectively. It seems the company is in its heavy investment phase right now.

The company’s other key efficiency and profitability metrics are also on an uptrend since its bottom line is growing. RDCM’s ROE and ROA are decent, just over my minimum of 10% and 5%, respectively; however, the company’s return on total capital is quite low at around 4%. I usually look for at least 10% ROTC, but it could be the case that the business segment it operates in simply cannot achieve this.

If we look at the company’s peers to get a broader look at the business overall, we can see that even with a 4% ROTC, the company is ahead of the pack and by a long mile. So, it is not unusual in this context that it is so low. Mind you, these peers were selected by default by Seeking Alpha. They may not be equal but are all in the same sector of Information Technology and Application Software industry, as well as have similar market caps.

Since we’re on the topic of financial statements, the company’s cash position in the most recent quarter stood at around $16m in cash and $91m in short-term bank deposits. The company also has no long-term debt apart from some operating lease obligations, which makes the company’s balance sheet very healthy. The company could use all that cash to continue to grow its operations or reward its shareholders somehow, as it seems like a lost opportunity to hoard all that cash right now. On the other hand, a massive cash cushion will help it weather any sort of downturn if we get one.

Overall, it seems like the company’s operations have been doing well. All of the important metrics I looked at are in an uptrend, and during the same time, the total returns have been decent if you were an investor for the last two years. The total return has outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) by a decent mile. It seems that the current CEO, Benny Eppstein, and the outgoing CFO, Hadar Rahav, both of whom have recently taken the reins in the last year, have done a tremendous job of turning the company around. But was this just a fluke, and the management got lucky with the influx of capex spending by many companies, driving RDCM’s sales inevitably higher? The share price performance has been quite lackluster, with around 13% up, just matching the SPY’s performance.

Comments on the Outlook

Let’s be honest, the company’s past share price performance has not been particularly exciting. If we zoom back out, we can see that a massive correction happened in the summer of 2018, when one of its biggest customers was hit by a massive typhoon, erasing a huge part of the company’s valuation. The company didn’t find any impact on its operations, yet the stock never quite recovered from such a massive down move. Not only that, but the top management of the company got restructured. The company hasn’t recovered yet.

It seems, however, the company’s position is indeed improving. And it looks like it is all thanks to the nature of companies wanting to continue to grow and expand their operations, which means upgrading to the newest technology there is. This time around, it is, of course, the hyper-growth story of AI. Many enterprises, including telecoms, but especially big tech, are spending billions in AI-related capex, and it is only natural that Radcom is going to follow the money with its software updates that are specifically designed for AI workloads, making everything a lot more efficient for its customers.

The company’s flagship product, the RADCOM ACE, has been around for many years, and in the last few years, has been updated to include the latest technology that is all the buzz right now. Cloud and data center revenues have been growing tremendously since the world was affected by the 2019 pandemic. For reference, pre-pandemic cloud computing revenue was around $255B in 2019 and is projected to exceed more than $720B this year. Enterprise spending has been the key revenue contributor for many companies relying on B2B channels, and that is not going to change any time soon, since most large tech companies are looking to spend billions more in 2026 compared to 2025, and those numbers were already out of this world. It just wouldn’t be fair to compare the company’s business to what it was just 7 years ago. The technology has progressed so much that it looks like there is still going to be quite a bit of growth left. Data centers, cloud, and AI are all the buzzwords that the company is focusing on, so it would be very hard to mess this up. I was actually very surprised that when the company announced its partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) to develop a next-generation high-capacity data capture and analytics solution, which would be powered by NVDA’s BlueField-3 DPU, its shares didn’t magically skyrocket as with many other partnerships that involved NVDA in any shape or form.

Global 5G and mobile data traffic is still growing rapidly, which means there will be a higher demand for automated monitoring and assurance as companies scale their operations. With decent software in its hands, RDCM should be able to win future contracts to drive revenue growth further, or to at least maintain 17% CAGR for a little while longer. Many operators are transforming their operations by moving on to the cloud infrastructure to cut down on OpEx and improve quality, which means RDCM’s TAM is expanding too, as its RADCOME ACE software becomes a lot more desirable.

So, in terms of macroeconomy, the company just needs to keep riding the AI hype train to continue to improve its operations and top-line growth. There are things it can do to drive sustainable growth going forward from within and not solely rely on macro drivers. The company already has solid relationships with Tier 1 customers like AT&T (T) and Rakuten (RKUNY) Mobile. The company needs to maintain this momentum and try to expand its reach with other Tier 1 telecoms, and there are many. As long as it can demonstrate that its product is superior to the competition, this should be no problem. Execution is key. Having customers like T and Rakuten, as well as DISH, should be viewed as the company’s product creating value. I expect more future partnerships to be announced.

Valuation

I went ahead and made a valuation model for the company to see what kind of price an investor should pay. It is not too conservative or optimistic in my opinion. Below are the assumptions I made.

For revenues, I am modelling around 17% CAGR for the next 5 years, which I think is achievable given it has been around this number for a while now. Non-GAAP operating margin was around 21% this past quarter, so I am assuming it will increase slightly to 23% over the next 5 years, which is also reasonable given the company’s high gross margins and further efficiency improvements due to AI and cloud demand. The rest of the inputs I’m leaving more or less close to the company’s historical averages, except for the tax rate, I don’t think 4% was reasonable, and the company’s WACC of around 6.8% was not enough margin of safety for me, so I went with 10% as my discount rate. With these assumptions, we get the EPS projection below.

With these assumptions, we get the company’s intrinsic value to be $14.79 a share, meaning it is currently trading at a slight discount to its fair value.

Investor Takeaway

I think if you already own the company and owned it through the down times, as it saw very recently, there is no need for you to sell right now. The company will most likely continue to grow at a decent pace as more and more telecoms transition their operations to the more advanced solutions, and since the AI hype is stronger than ever, 2026 looks like a good year for the company.

For me, however, it is not too enticing currently. The main reason is that I am still in a rebalancing stage of my portfolio because my AI plays have become a bit overweighted, and I am looking to hedge some of that exposure over the next while. RDCM is pretty much AI, cloud, and data center play, which has been the hottest sector recently, and I don’t feel comfortable adding more to the exuberance; therefore, I will avoid it for now.

data.title: ADX Vs. USA: Why I Am Buying The Winner And Selling The Loser
data.description:
Investment thesis

The quest for assets that generate high returns in the form of dividend payments continues. And today, I will take a look at two of the most legendary closed-end funds with a long history: Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) and Liberty All-Star Equity Fund (USA). The seemingly similar CEFs are completely different, concealing their fundamental differences in strategy philosophy. Where ADX functions as a highly efficient “hidden index fund” managed by a single team, the USA fund employs a more complex multi-manager approach that reduces dependence on the S&P 500 (SPY) index by targeting a combination of value and growth strategies.

In 2025, that fundamental difference between the funds leads to a dramatic gap in their results. While the ADX demonstrates share price growth in line with the market, having a high total return, USA struggles to achieve its goals, showing negative dynamics and widening the discount to its net asset value. My goal in this article is to do a comparative analysis of these two funds to answer the question: Why is ADX a "Buy" while USA is a "Sell" candidate? In spite of the USA's higher stated yield, I make a pragmatic choice in favor of ADX, investing, essentially, into the same S&P 500, but at some discount and with a guaranteed minimum payout of 8% per annum.

Fundamental similarities between ADX and USA strategies and their main differences

The ADX and USA are fundamentally similar closed-end funds, some of the oldest in their field, and have very similar payouts. ADX is required to pay a minimum of 8% of NAV per year (previously 6%), whereas USA is required to pay 10%. The management of these funds differs, as ADX is managed by a single team, whereas USA is managed using a multi-manager approach. That means that the fund's assets are distributed among five different management companies, 3 of which focus on value and 2 on growth.

Despite the fact that ADX and USA are "twin brothers" in terms of investment strategy, but they have different management mechanics. At first sight, these closed-end funds may seem very similar to each other. However, deep fundamental analysis reveals several points that distinguish them. As I have already mentioned, they have a different approach to management. That fundamental difference is the main distinction, because the portfolio structure of the funds depends on it.

Because ADX is managed by a single team, they chose one market segment in which to invest capital. It is the S&P 500 because ADX results correlate 95% with changes in the price of the main market benchmark. Fund managers do not try to predict new stars and growth catalysts; they trust the market and select only the best assets in their opinion. That is why the ADX portfolio can be called an improved version of the S&P500 index. And the main share in it is occupied by assets of the information technology sector (34.5% of the entire portfolio). Correspondingly, the aim of such a fund strategy is to achieve market results with minimal costs, converting capital growth into the generation of a stable stream of payments.

As USA is managed by five different external management companies, their strategy is completely different, because there is clear diversification here. Three of the portfolio managers follow a value strategy, building a portfolio of undervalued assets. While the other two portfolio managers build a portfolio of growth stocks. The purpose of such multi-manager portfolio investment management is to attempt to smooth out market volatility. By design, if growth stocks fall, the value stocks should stabilize the results to a certain median. But in fact, it is not so, because as the current market situation shows, the intended action in 2025 leads to an overall loss in the results of the USA portfolio. Moreover, the IT sector still accounts for the largest share of the portfolio (26.9% of the total portfolio), though Financials and Health Care are in second and third place, which, in my opinion, are the reasons for the negative stock performance in 2025.

When it comes to commission policy, the ADX offers an advantage, because its 0.59% commission is way lower than the USA's 1.01%. The difference of 0.42% in commission can really eat into investors' capital over the long run, making the ADX a solid choice. Plus, that fund has historically traded at a discount in the 10-15% range (though now it's closer to 5-10%), making it a more attractive buy. The 10% discount to NAV means you can buy assets worth $1 for 90 cents. With USA, its stock has historically traded at or slightly below par. Since April 2025, though, the fund has started to see a serious discount, widening to -9.72% today. This situation is way different from what we've seen before, and it doesn't make USA seem any more reliable.

The primary quantitative indicator (Total Return) signals a significant difference between the returns of ADX and USA. The first fund has historically benefited from the fact that its portfolio value has been driven by the growth of IT giants. USA's portfolio consists of assets classified as value stocks, due to which value stocks have underperformed growth stocks. Hence, against the backdrop of a prolonged global bullish trend, the USA has emerged as the loser. This is reflected in the results for the past year, where the result is entirely negative.

Therefore, while USA has a dividend yield of 10.93% for the year, the Total Return is negative (-6.85%). This is because the fund's share price has declined, whereas ADX shows the opposite upward price movement. Meanwhile, in spite of dividend payments of 8.01% per annum (which is 2.92% less than in the USA), the Total Return is 20.82%. This performance gap can be seen over both a 3-year and a 10-year period. As an example, over the last 3 years, Total Return ADX was 103.15%, whereas for the USA it was only 37.66%. The difference of 2.73 times makes investing in ADX much more profitable.

Turning to the differences in dividend payment policies, it should be noted that ADX and USA are funds whose purpose, based on their prospectuses, is to turn stock growth into dividends. But despite this, their payment characteristics differ. This is not only due to the payment obligations themselves (8% of NAV for ADX and 10% for USA). It should also be noted that the fourth and final dividend payment for the year at ADX varies, because it is "equalizing" and includes the successes of the current year. Whereas at USA, it is strictly a payment of 2.5% of NAV each quarter. Also worth noting is that ADX has less predictable income, due to which payments may vary by the end of the year. Whereas USA payments are more predictable, with the amount changing smoothly in line with the net asset price. I also want to add that in practice, it is possible that USA's dividends are taxed as ROC. It happens in periods when the year has been bad.

Comparative table of ADX and USA fund characteristics

The following is a brief comparative table of key criteria allowing you to determine the difference between ADX and USA. I can only name one advantage of USA: lower concentration of the top 10 assets in the portfolio structure, thus increasing risk diversification, and higher dividend yield. But these criteria don't reflect the actual result in terms of Total Return.

Criteria

Risks of the investment thesis

Since in the comparison between ADX and USA, for me, the former fund is the clear leader, I will look at the risks of investing in this particular asset. The main feature, and among other things, the drawback, is that it's highly correlated with the S&P 500 index. That is why the overall return on ADX can be affected by all the fundamental factors that influence the economic situation on the US stock market. This includes macroeconomic statistics and the Fed's monetary policy agenda. And most importantly, the results of ADX can be negatively affected if the growth of Mag-7 shares stops. In that case, relying on the Growth investment strategy will lead to problems, including secured payments, because of which the net asset value will begin to decline. If we mention the USA, in this fund, these problems are minimized through diversification of strategies, with a portfolio focused on Value stocks.

Conclusion

In summary, results of comparative analysis of fundamental differences between ADX and USA reveal minimal similarities between them, whereas there are numerous differences. However, I see ADX as a clear favorite, despite its lower dividend yield, having significantly better results in terms of Total Return from 1 to 10 years. For a long period of time, the results of ADX have exceeded those of USA, due to which I recommend investing in the former fund with a "Buy" rating. Whereas for USA, my recommendation is to "Sell", as my bet on investing in Value stocks has not paid off. For the first time in a long time, the USA discount to NAV reached almost 10%. Such dynamics reflect investors' loss of faith in high dividends from this fund, which might coincide with the negative dynamics of changes in net asset value. Choosing ADX for your portfolio is a pragmatic choice, as you are essentially investing in the same S&P 500, but you are buying it at a discount. On top of that, you are getting a "forced" profit of 8% per annum. This deal is really good math-wise, as it can boost your capital growth over the long run because of the low fees.

data.title: Rates Spark: Funds Rate Now Below 10yr SOFR
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas and Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

Finally, we have the funds rate below 10yr SOFR – now that’s more normal

The recent FOMC meetings share a theme: rates are converging toward what appears to be a landing zone on several fronts, even if only temporarily. The balance sheet is now on freeze (there is a floor set for bank reserves), and the funds rate is now close to where it is looking more normal, or at least close to it. Let’s delve into both.

First, the level of the funds rate and floating rates in general. The 3mth SOFR rate is finally below the 10yr SOFR rate (just this week). Which means, at long last, it’s cheaper to fund floating than to fund fixed. This is with the 10yr SOFR having been under rising pressure in the past weeks, but also as the 3mth SOFR has been tamed by the rate-cutting narrative. It’s the first time since the second half of 2022 (and briefly in January 2025) that the 10yr SOFR rate has been above most floating rates, and that will be copper-fastened as soon as the Fed cuts by 25bp in a bit. For players who have baulked at the idea of swapping to floating on account of negative impact carry, that has now gone away (at least for as long as 10yr SOFR remains elevated).

Second, liquidity conditions. Repo has been trending tight in the past few months, and things reached a point of no return when the effective funds rate began to de-anchor and trek towards the rate on excess reserves (now just 1bp below). The Fed reacted at the previous FOMC meeting by reverse-engineering this into a rationale for freezing the balance sheet, where further MBS roll-offs would be matched by T-bills buying. But as we’ve noted, the Fed will ultimately have to re-expand the balance sheet at the same pace as the nominal economy is growing. So, if nominal GDP is growing at 3-5%, bank reserves too should expand at that rate. The Fed has reacted to this reality by implementing flexibility to buy T-bills in excess of the MBS roll-off, to ensure ample reserves.

Reserve management purchases is the term the Fed is using to describe its T-bill buying agenda, as in fact they can go out as far as three years in Treasuries if needed. That’s quite deviant from a pure T- bills buying programme, and bordering in fact on QE. They’ll start with $40bn of buying, and suggest it will wane in later months due to seasonal liquidity circumstances. Good for the front end!

Thursday's events and market views

From Europe, we have little notable data, but the US should publish trade balance data for September and also the weekly jobless claims. Consensus sees the jobless claims rising to 220k from the previous 191k reading. Furthermore, the Bank of England's Andrew Bailey will speak about financial stability.

In terms of issuance, we have Italy with 3Y, 4Y and 5Y BTPs for a total of €5bn. The US will auction a 30Y Bond totalling $22bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
