data.title: Apple: Late To The AI Party, Wait And See But Never Count It Out
data.description:
This company needs no introduction. Apple (AAPL) is the original Silicon Valley company.

It turns 50 next year and in its half-century of operation it has almost always been on the cutting edge of personal computing. Apple introduced the world to color graphics, the first tablet computer, and the modern smartphone.

But now, Apple has fallen behind, even though it might not seem like it at first.

The iPhone is still a perennial bestseller with control over 18% of the global smartphone market and almost 60% of the American smartphone market. It also still has some of the highest customer loyalty in the game with a 92% retention rate for iPhone users…

However, Apple's name has been noticeably absent from headlines surrounding the next big thing in tech today; AI. Until recently, that is. But we'll get to that.

Apple has Apple Intelligence, but it has serious problems that keep it from being in the same league as. Alphabet's (Nasdaq: GOOG) Gemini or Microsoft's (Nasdaq: MSFT) Copilot. And in the last year it has lagged behind several of its Magnificent Seven peers with a return of 10.84% to Microsoft's 16.3%, Nvidia's (NVDA) 37.8%, and Alphabet's 64.7%

But Apple can't be counted out just yet. The company has been through far worse than being behind the eight-ball before. It was weeks from declaring bankruptcy in 1996 before Steve Jobs came back to the company he co-founded.

Apple is a hold for me. But despite its slow adaptation to AI, I think Apple could emerge as the dark horse in the AI race.

Let's get into it…

The AI Dark Horse

Of any of the companies I've covered, Apple needs no explanation. Everybody knows what Apple does. There's a decent chance you're reading this on an Apple product. In many ways, Apple invented the modern tech industry.

The first smartphone and tablet computer were both from Apple. Even going back further than that, the Apple II was the first computer adopted in American public schools back in the 1980s.

Yet, despite its history as an innovator, Apple is lagging behind its perennial rival Microsoft in the AI arms race. But Apple has pulled itself back from far worse situations before and it's a company you can never count out.

The biggest news on the AI front for Apple recently was John Giannandrea, Apple's former head of artificial intelligence stepping down to serve as an advisor until he formally retires in spring 2026.

Stepping into Giannandrea's former role is Amar Subramanya who has previously worked on AI projects for both Microsoft and Alphabet. That on its own is promising, Subramanya is young at 46 but he has an impressive resume…

Most recently he served as Microsoft's VP of AI and worked on the models underpinning Microsoft Copilot. Before that he spent 16 years at Google, eventually becoming Gemini's head of engineering. So, there are likely few better people Apple could have tapped to jumpstart its AI program.

And I actually think Apple being a bit behind in AI now has serious potential to work in its benefit in the long run. The company's approach to AI is fundamentally different from its peers in the Magnificent Seven and I think that, combined with being able to watch other companies make mistakes developing their AI, will allow Apple to emerge as a dark horse in AI.

And what do I mean by mistakes?

First and most tragically is the wrongful death lawsuit brought against OpenAI by the family of Zane Shamblin. There are also numerous lawsuits in several states against AI companies surrounding copyright issues.

There's the issue of pornographic content made of people without their consent using generative AI models. In June, San Francisco's city attorney ordered the shutdown of 10 websites that used AI-generated adult content with explicit images of real adults and minors.

Because so many tech companies focused on AI have landed themselves in legal hot water, I would be shocked if Apple didn't learn from those mistakes and handle the development of its own AI with great care.

It's also worth considering that Apple has generally shown a commitment to user privacy.

Most recently the company protested an attempt by the Indian government to pre-load a state-owned security app onto all smartphones and actually got the state to backtrack. If you're a mildly paranoid individual online like I am, that's a point in Apple's favor.

Finally, I want to address the matter of how Apple handles its AI hardware development. While many other tech companies are building out their own data center capacity, a move that is very capital-intensive, Apple is choosing to avoid that same capital expenditure.

According to Dgtl Infra, a new data center can run between $7 million to $12 million per megawatt of load. Small datacenters usually run in the 1-5 MW range while larger ones can hit 20 MW or even over 100 MW.

So, let's take a point in the middle of the cost range, say $10 million/MW for easy math. That puts us at $10-$50 million for a small datacenter, $20 million for a 20 MW center or $100 million for a 100 MW center. Put simply, they aren't cheap.

Instead of building data centers, Apple buys computing capacity from other companies and saves the data centers (which it uses its own chips in, not Nvidia or AMD hardware) for its Private Cloud Compute service.

This is a much leaner way of running and developing AI which allowed Apple to spend just $12.72 billion in capital expenditures for its fiscal 2025 compared to the $34.9 billion spent by Microsoft (MSFT), the $71 billion spent by Meta (META) or the $92 billion spent by Alphabet (GOOG) in their most recent fiscal years.

So, already we see Apple avoiding the cash sink of building its own vast array of data centers and knowing which pitfalls to avoid from a legal standpoint.

And don't worry about Apple having the means to build its AI capabilities out quickly. Even without a flagship AI product, the iPhone remains a bestseller globally and one glance at its balance sheet shows Apple is far from the cash strapped place it was in 1996. Let's take a look…

Bargaining From a Position of Strength

As you'd expect, per its full-year 2025 results, released in October, Apple is doing great. Revenue for 2025 grew 6.4% to $416.1 billion, net income grew 19.45% to $112 billion, cash and cash equivalents grew 20% to $35.9 billion, and total liabilities fell 7.3%, and EPS surged 22.5% over 2024.

The company's gross margin is sitting at 46.91% and the net income margin is 26.92% which is five times higher than the median in its sector. Year to date Apple has lagged, only growing 10.84%, but I think that's more due to the lack of AI headlines driving price speculation than anything to do with Apple's actual performance which remains fantastic.

And it's no wonder, Apple's products have some serious customer loyalty and they're high quality. The iPhone 17 blew the doors off the product's last sales record in 2021 with the iPhone 13. Apple will ship 247.4 million iPhones this year, up 6% year over year.

Apple's PCs remain a strong competitor in the crowded laptop and desktop space, holding 4th place behind Lenovo, HP, and Dell. But Apple is growing its market share there with Mac sales growth outpacing PCs at a rate of 14.9% to 8.1% in Q3 2025 (the latest figure I could find comparing sales across computer brands.)

Point blank, Apple simply has the money to throw at developing its own AI products and it's doing it in a far smarter and less capital-intensive way than its competitors. And while its P/E ratio is higher than the sector median, 37.32 to 24.87, it's in line with but still higher than its Magnificent Seven peers like Alphabet at 31.76 and Microsoft at 33.39, so while it's not great and I'd like to see it lower before Apple becomes a buy for me, I'm also not particularly worried about it.

Apple's RSI is sitting at 69 which is higher than I usually like to see but the company routinely goes into the mid-high 70s range before it pulls back a bit. Use your discretion when timing your purchase if you choose to get in now, and aim for when that number is closer to 40 or 50.

But, in the long term, Apple has consistently gone up so the longer you hold the shares any slight difference in dollar-cost average will smooth out. But I still don't think now is the time to get in. Its P/E is higher than both of its peers that already have AI products and the company has shown nothing concrete in AI aside from putting a new person in charge of it, so it's not AI hype inflating Apple's value.

Still, Apple is doing pretty well even without a direct competitor to Gemini or Copilot, it's swimming in cash, it's got growing revenue and earnings, solid cash reserves to throw at its AI product development, and manageable debt. It's a hold for me but not far from a buy. I just need to see something concrete on the AI front that pans out for Apple to change my recommendation.

Now, lets take a look at the positive case, and why being late to the AI party right now isn't necessarily a bad thing…

The Potential Catalyst

There's endless debate in the financial media over whether or not there's an AI bubble forming. We can debate it all day but I'm of a mind that there is indeed a bubble, and I think it could pop as soon as next year.

The most damning evidence for it in my book is the MIT study from earlier this year which found that, in spite of investments of $30 to $40 billion in generative AI, a full 95% of organizations saw a net return of diddly squat on their investment.

The second piece of evidence is history. Every overhyped technology has created a bubble that burst at some point. The Dotcom bubble in the 1990s and the Bitcoin bubble of the 2010s predict the AI bubble of the 2020s.

That's not to say AI has no value, it certainly does, just like the internet and cryptocurrency before it. But hype always drives too much speculation until the bubble pops and lots of people are left holding the bag.

Now if I'm right and the AI bubble does pop, then the companies most heavily invested in AI are going to get hit the hardest. And Apple is the biggest tech company with the least investment in AI.

And, given that Apple has a much leaner and less cash-intensive way of developing its AI programs I think it will be able to swoop in after the bubble pops with a product developed by avoiding the mistakes made by its competitors.

Most companies adopting AI are still experimenting with it and haven't adopted one particular AI model to standardize on so the market is still open to new competition.

Apple has a great opportunity here if things go right for it, but there's also the chance any new AI product it rolls out in the near future will be too little too late.

The Potential Risks

Now, while many companies are only in the experimental stage of AI adoption, that hasn't stopped a few of them from eating up market share at a rapid pace. Back in 2023 OpenAI was the dominant player in the Enterprise LLM API market with 50% share all on its lonesome.

But in the two years since, OpenAI's share of the market has plummeted to 25% while Anthropic and Google have gained, growing from 12%-32% and 7%-20% respectively. Anthropic (the company behind Claude) also dominates the coding AI market with 42% share to OpenAI's 21% in second place.

The longer Apple waits to release a competitor to Google especially, the more I think Anthropic and Google will solidify their lead. Both companies have serious momentum and if they emerge as the clear leaders the odds of companies standardizing on their AI products go up, then the market will be harder for Apple to break into.

So, while Apple does have time, it can't wait too long or the market will consolidate and crystallize and Apple will struggle to dislodge some dug-in market leaders. Really, whether Apple becomes a buy for me depends entirely on their timing and the quality of their potential AI product.

If an Apple competitor to Claude or Gemini comes correctly and at the right time I think it will carve out a solid niche for itself in the market, particularly with other Apple hardware. But if Apple waits too long or it has another flop like Apple Intelligence then it will only struggle more to break in as the market matures.

The Verdict: Am I a Mac? Am I a PC?

Apple might be down in terms of AI but it's not out. Still, given its relatively high valuation compared to its Magnificent 7 peers and its promising but very early moves to revamp its AI program, it's a hold for me for now.

I am very close to being bullish on Apple, I would need to see its P/E ratio come down closer to its peers and see it make progress on AI. I'm watching and waiting on Apple but I think the odds are good that whatever competitor to Gemini and Copilot its cooking up will be impressive.

I also like that Apple is taking a less capital intensive approach to building out its AI infrastructure compared to its rivals, its balance sheet is fantastic, and all around it's one or two right moves away from being a buy.

So, I'm not a Mac, I'm not a PC, but I am an investor and Apple is almost there. I think it could very well return to its former position as the innovator of Silicon Valley, setting trends that everyone else scrambles to follow… But it's not there yet.

Wait and see on Apple, I know I will be.

data.title: Alliant Energy: Buy The Dip On AI Data Center Ramp
data.description:
Shares of Midwest utility company Alliant Energy (LNT) have recently followed a sell-off in the broad utility sector - as measured by the SPDR Utility Sector ETF (XLU) - due to fears of higher interest rates and therefore higher borrowing costs and reduced profitability. As a result, Alliant's stock is down ~$4/share from its 52-week high of $69.75 set late last month (see chart below). Today, I'll explain why investors should BUY the dip in this 3.1% yielding utility on the basis of go-forward margin expansion and multiple agreements to supply power to Google and QTS data centers in Iowa.

Investment Thesis

Wisconsin-based Alliant Energy operates two primary subsidiaries: Interstate Power & Light ("IPL") and Wisconsin Power & Light ("WLP"). The company serves ~1 million electric customers and 430,000 natural gas customers. The company also operates a wind farm in Oklahoma, a natural gas plant in Wisconsin, and has ownership interest in multiple electric power transmission businesses.

IPL provides natural gas distribution, electricity generation, and electricity distribution to customers in Iowa. It also sells electricity to customers in Iowa, Illinois, and Minnesota via the wholesale power market. WPL provides electricity, electric power & natural gas distribution, and wholesale electric power to customers in Wisconsin.

For full-year 2024, 85% of IPL's revenue was electric and 12% was gas. Similarly for WPL, 89% of revenue was electric and 12% was gas. A slide from Alliant's November Investor Presentation summarizes the company's operations:

In the lower left of the slide above, you can see that Alliant is a somewhat under-the-radar and under-appreciated top-5 renewable wind & solar operator with a 34.6% renewable share of the company's total 2024 electric power generation supply. The company continues to retire high-cost coal generation and replace it with nat gas, wind, and battery backup power capacity. Regulators in Wisconsin & Iowa have been embracing renewable energy and providing additional growth incentives via favorable rate-making decisions.

The investment opportunity in Alliant is threefold: first, IPL & WPL enjoy near monopoly rights for retail electric & gas operations in Wisconsin and Iowa. Second, LNT is expanding margins. Third, and most important here, expectations are for energy demand for Alliant to grow 50% from 2024 levels by 2030 as data center demand accelerates.

With that as background, let's take a look at the company's most recent earnings.

Earnings

The slide below is from LNT's Q3 presentation:

As you can see from the graphic, first nine months earnings of $2.62 were +12.5% yoy. The midpoint of updated FY2025 earnings ($3.20/share) would equate to a 19% increase as compared to the $2.69/share LNT earned in FY24. The midpoint of LNT's earnings guidance for FY26 is $3.41/share, which would be a 6.6% yoy increase.

EBIT margins should reach ~25-27% in 2025 (up from 22.3% in FY24) and toward the higher end of that range in 2026. Margin expansion is supported by ongoing rigorous maintenance cost control as well as better returns on invested capital.

Dividends

Alliant expects to maintain its 60-70% dividend payout ratio and has guided for a FY26 dividend target of $2.14/share. That would be a 5.4% increase and equates to a 3.3% at the current pixel-time price of $64.89.

Going Forward

Driving accelerated earnings & dividend growth going forward are growth in the rate base, favorable regulatory outcomes, and - of course - electricity demand from the ramp-up of AI data centers:

Data Centers

Indeed, Alliant has already started construction on three new data centers and reports it is in "active negotiations" for an additional 2-4 GW of additional capacity. This is the primary reason behind LNT's expectations for a rather remarkable 50% increase in demand by 2030 off of a 2024 base.

Iowa's Governor Kim Reynolds said the QTS data center is the single largest investment in the state's history. QTS will pay for all energy infrastructure costs, and there is expected to be no negative impact on consumers. Meantime, both Iowa & Wisconsin have very attractive attributes that will likely continue to attract AI data center customers:

In June, Alliant announced Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) decision to build a second data center development in Iowa - this time in Cedar Rapids (the first was Google's massive data center in Council Bluffs). Both Google and QTS will be located at the Big Cedar Industrial Center site. QTS had previously announced their project in February 2025. On the Q3 conference call, Alliant Executive VP & CFO Robert Durian reported additional (bullish) details on the Google project:

Yes. I think of that as of the 3 gigawatts, it's about 300 megawatts in total. And yes, they were interested in just going faster. I'll go back to my earlier comments. You'll see some of that starting to come in, in the second half of 2026, and then just going to ramp-up quicker than we originally anticipated. So you'll see more load in '27 and '28 than we originally expected.

Valuation

Alliant Energy currently trades at a TTM P/E=20.4x, a forward P/E=20.2x, and a yield of 3.1%. That compares with the XLU Utility ETF's P/E=21.0x and 2.6% yield. From that standpoint, Alliant's 50 bps higher dividend yield as compared to the broad utility sector is quite attractive, especially considering the relatively bullish outlook for revenue & dividend growth moving forward.

Risks

Higher interest rates pose a risk to all utility companies, and Alliant is no exception. As I pointed out in a recent Seeking Alpha article, despite the fact that President Trump is putting severe pressure on the Federal Reserve to significantly lower interest rates, the long end of the bond market isn't really cooperating with that narrative given that inflation is still closer to 3% than the Fed's 2% target (see: "Where To Invest $10,000 After Your Year-End Portfolio Review"). We can see the impact of the current interest rate environment by noting WPL's recent $300 million offering of 5.7% debentures due in 2055. Higher interest rates and/or inflation can reduce profitability. This is likely one reason behind the recent pullback in utility stocks.

General economic weakness in Wisconsin & Iowa could negatively impact demand for LNT's electric power demand growth going forward.

In addition, there is the possibility that the data center build-out does not realize current expectations. That would reduce LNT's growth rate - both in terms of revenue and dividends - and would likely cause a lower multiple valuation.

Summary & Conclusion

Alliance Energy operates in an extremely attractive region of the country for AI data center projects, and that is resulting in several data center power supply wins for LNT with several more large projects under discussion. I expect additional bullish AI data center announcements in 2026. As a result, the company expects power demand to grow a whopping 50% by 2030 off a 2024 base. In the meantime, Alliant is enjoying some meaningful margin expansion to go along with the outlook for accelerating revenue & dividend growth. Alliant's current dividend yield of 3.1% is attractive and 50 bps higher than the broad utility sector XLU ETF's dividend yield of 2.6%.

Put it all together, and Alliant Energy is a BUY.

data.title: Trade Tensions And AI: The Big Market Themes For 2026
data.description:
With U.S.-China trade and AI potentially set to be the dominant market themes in 2026, how should investors be preparing? MoneyTalk discusses with Kevin Hebner, Global Investment Strategist with TD Epoch.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: US-China trade tensions and the boom in artificial intelligence development have been the two dominant market themes this year. So what should investors expect heading into 2026? Joining us now to discuss, Kevin Hebner, global market strategist with TD Epoch.

Kevin, great to have you back on the show.

Kevin Hebner: Great. Thanks, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: Lots to talk about. It's been quite a year. China challenging the US this year. Those tensions are real. You think tensions perhaps could be a bit lower next year? How could it all play out?

Kevin Hebner: So, we had two times this year where there's an escalation of the tensions-- in April, after Liberation Day, where Trump announced his tariffs across the globe, and then in October, as Trump tightened export controls on advanced chips and then China responded with rare earth export controls or the threat of. And I think those are very important events. But going into 2026, the two leaders are meeting three times, starting in April, then November, December. So there's going to be lots of chance to discuss.

And given they both love photo ops, I think there's a high probability that we'll see some type of narrow trade deal, but something that I think will lead markets to, I think, relax a little bit-- I think will be especially positive for Chinese equities. Because a lot of investors have viewed Chinese equities as uninvestable. But once we see a type of truce between the two countries-- the G2 countries-- I think that's going to be good, especially for Chinese equities.

Greg Bonnell: You've been here many times during the year. And during those periods of tension, you talked about who had escalation dominance. Walk us through that and what the potential end game is here for these two powers.

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So escalation dominance was especially important in October. And so China has spent decades getting dominance in a host of important sectors. For example, they have over 80% share in production of electric vehicles, of drones, of batteries, of rare earths. And so, they've started to weaponize this dominance that they have.

The US also has dominance in a number of areas-- for example, airplanes, advanced semiconductors, the global financial system, access to the US dollar-- everything is based there. And then access to the US consumer. And for Chinese leaders, particularly the elite leaders, they want their kids going to the top US universities. They control access to that.

So they both have areas they can escalate. But [what] we saw in October was, the big difference is pain asymmetry-- is President Xi, in particular, he suffered a lot growing up. His dad was treated pretty brutally by Mao. And for a while, President Xi actually lived in a cave. And President Xi seems to think that young Chinese people haven't suffered enough and that, to some extent, pain is good. It builds character. Where President Trump-- there's always another election, we're always raising money, and no tolerance for any type of pain that could lead to layoffs and bad news.

So, Trump folded at the end of October. And I think this is one reason to be constructive, because Trump learned a lot from that, and I don't think we're going to see the type of escalation we saw this year. And anytime we do see markets selling off on fears of escalation between the two countries, I think that's a real opportunity to buy.

Greg Bonnell: OK. So let's talk about if we get to this point with China and the US where they start to decide that there's little wins they can have for each other, where does Canada fit into all this? Where can we start to improve our relationships?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So I think that's important. So, if you look at the sectors where the US is vulnerable now-- and it's a long list of sectors. So it includes-- we mentioned EVs, drones, batteries, rare earth materials, steel, aluminum, shipbuilding. It's a lot of sectors where the US is vulnerable. And Trump's number one economic priority is to reindustrialize the United States to reduce these vulnerabilities.

And Trump realized at the end of October that he can't do this on his own. And so, prior to October, he often treated allies worse than adversaries. And there's been a real change in tone. There's been a real pivot from him since the end of October. So this is an opportunity for Canada. I think Carney does need to build a relationship with Trump. And I don't think that's going so well.

But to identify these potential win-wins, how Canada can help America with the challenges they face-- so rare earths is a pretty obvious one, aluminum, steel. There's issues with Algoma (ASTL) now. There's opportunities with steel, with shipbuilding, particularly icebreakers-- Canada's number two producer of icebreakers in the world after Russia. And it's sort of Arctic defense, NATO broadly. AI research-- Canada's one of the leaders of the world through the various universities and the top people who are at University of Toronto, Waterloo, Montreal, and so forth.

So there's a lot of win-wins. And I think rather than have an elbows-up approach, it's more, let's work together. Let's be partners. Let's be a trusted ally and work on these areas.

Greg Bonnell: Alright. So that's trade, and that's politics-- the big theme. The other big theme of the year, of course, artificial intelligence, really couldn't turn your head for a second-- or at least I couldn't turn my head for a second-- without having to talk about it here on the show. Let's talk about some of the hyperscalers. What's the outlook there?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So the big issue with the hyperscalers, and AI in general, is the amount of CapEx that they've been spending over the last year. And it's expected by the companies to be over 300 billion in 2026. That's up 6x from what it was in 2020. We have a chart up here showing that now.

And it's interesting. So what is $320 billion? What does that number mean? That's over 10% of Canadian GDP that's being spent in one year. And that's just three companies. If you included more companies, it'd be about 15% of Canadian GDP. And if you look at other big projects by the US government-- the Apollo project, which ran over a decade-- it's twice that amount of money. That's in today's dollars. Manhattan Project, that was five years-- it's 8 times that. So it's an enormous amount of money.

And our view is that this all makes sense. AI is going to be transformational. Industrial robots is going to be the biggest industry ever. But it's going to take a lot longer than people expect. In particular, one key theme now is what's going on with OpenAI (OPENAI). So OpenAI is valued at $500 billion, according to its last fundraise. But it has a consumer chatbot that is probably not as good as the products from Anthropic or Google. And it's being challenged by the open-source models from China.

So it looks like a bit of a commodity. How is it going to monetize it? And OpenAI is committed to spending tens and tens of billions of dollars without commensurate revenues. So, I think it's reasonable to think, maybe in '26 or '27, that OpenAI starts to reduce their projections for spending, which takes a little bit of pressure off Anthropic so they can reduce their projections-- similar for Google and down the line. And so we continue to have strong growth numbers for AI CapEx, but less so than the numbers that we saw on the previous slide.

Because I think it's going to take much longer than people expect broadly for AI to result in products that can be monetized. So we're spending all this money now. But the returns of that money, I think, are further out than people expect. So there will be a down cycle. And I don't know whether that happens in '26 or '27 or '28-- you can never forecast these things. But I think that's going to be a big factor for markets.

Greg Bonnell: With all that money being spent, you think there could be a down cycle. A lot of these names have done quite well too, in terms of, like, market gains, share appreciation. People talk about a bubble. They fear a bubble. They start talking about 1999. How do you read the situation?

Kevin Hebner: So it's totally different than '99. '99, much of the spending was on telecoms and fiber optic cable. And in 1999, 90% of that cable was dark, it wasn't being used. So they were building supply ahead of demand. Right now, all of the data centers, all the chips are being used. In fact, there's enormous demand for any supply. And the bottleneck's really on the supply front.

Further, the term "bubble" is huge. It's broad. I think it's far too binary. I think there's a good chance there's an LLM bubble, because LLM is not the architecture for the future--

Greg Bonnell: The large language models.

Kevin Hebner: Yes. Particularly when you think about drones, EVs, or AVs, and robots. LLMs will have more world models, different architecture for that. And particular, in private markets with companies like OpenAI valued at $500 billion, or Anthropic (ANTHRO) at $300 billion, or xAI (X.AI), I think there's some real excesses there, and maybe that there's some companies in public markets who are investing a lot in data centers but are financing this through cash, whereas Google (GOOG, GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), and so on, they're financing through cash flow.

So I think there are areas that are probably ahead of themselves and we could see pullbacks. But for the big hyperscalers-- and a lot of the quality companies in the AI stack, they're producing so much cash, have great margins, great return on invested capital that maybe we could see a bit of choppiness, a bit of a pullback, but nothing resembling what we saw in 2000, 2001.

Greg Bonnell: OK. So with this as our backdrop, how should investors be positioning themselves, heading into a new year?

Kevin Hebner: Yes. So I think first thing is, stay invested. So I've been through, over my 30 years, a number of cycles. Nobody's smart enough to call the top and call the bottom. And so, for example, from 1995 to '05, you did very well staying invested in spite of the tech crash. Similar from '05 to '15, in spite of the GFC and so on. So stay invested. Stay invested in tech as well but quality tech companies, companies producing a lot of cash so that they're not very vulnerable.

Thirdly, I think it's important to consider diversifying outside the US. So, so many investors are concentrated in the US, in tech. And we know that the top 10 companies in the S&P represent 40% of market cap. So, there are global champions in the UK, France, Germany, Japan, even China-- companies like BYD (BYDDF, BYDDY) and CATL (CTATF) that I think should be interesting to investors.

I think another area to think about is infrastructure as an asset class. It's in a secular boom because of reindustrialization as well as AI. And it's not just ports and pipelines but all the data centers and the grid, the electric grid behind it. So, I think that's a very interesting area.

And finally, I would say the US dollar has had a very good run. It's expensive against every currency out there. And as I think we see the US government get more involved in economic policy, as I think we see the tensions in China stay at pretty elevated areas-- and certain aspects of Trump's economic policies will concern investors-- I would think that through the remainder of Trump's term, I would expect the US dollar trade-weighted to decline by, say, 10-15%.
